text: GRANDBOROUGH, a parish of Warwickshire, 3 m. S of Dunchurch. Area 4,100 acres. Pop. 532. -Also a parish in Bucks, 12 m. S of Winslow. Area 1,560 acres. Pop. 345.
GRAND-BOURG, or Marigor, a town on the SW coast of the island of Marie-Galante, of which it is the cap. Pop. 1,500.
GRAND-BOURG-SALAGNAC, a small town and canton of France, in the dep. of Creuse, arrond. and 14 m. W of Gueret. Pop. of town, 2,646; of 1 cant., 9,048.
GRAND CAMP, a town of France, in the dep. of Calvados, on the Channel, at the embouchure of the Vire.

GRAND CANAL, a great inland navigation, chiefly of Leinster and partly of Connaught. It extends W through co. Dublin, co. Kildare, King's co., and co. Galway, from Dublin to Ballinasloe, touching the towns of Edenderry, Philipstown, and Tullamore, and crossing the river Shannon at Shannon harbour; and it sends off branches to Naas, Athy, Portarlington, Mountmellick, Milltown, Blackwood, Ballyteigue, and Kilbeggan. A very large aggregate of even the original work and its earliest branches, traverses the main body and large detachments of the Bog of Allen; so that a trip to Dublin from Ballinasloe, along the canal, is, over the greater part of the distance, but a degree less gloomy than a journey of equal length through the Sahara. What may be termed the original canal, or the line from the Liffey near Ringsend to the Shannon near the mouth of the Brosna, and the branches to Athy, Milltown, Blackwood, Ballyteigue, and Edenderry, may be stated at 100 m. in length. Its rise from the Liffey to the summit-level is 278 ft.; its fall from the summit-level to the Shannon and the Barrow is respectively 162 and 97 ft.; the average amount of its lockage is 51 ft. per mile; and the cost of executing the work was £1,861,008,—equal to £18,610 per mile. The canal, as at present existing, consists of a main line of 79 m. from the Liffey to the Shannon, an extension of 15 m. to Ballinasloe, and seven ramifications of aggregately 653 m. in length, being a total length of <math>160\frac{1}{4}</math> m. The work is intimately connected, both topographically and economically, with the navigation of the Barrow, and with all the navigation, upper, middle, and lower, of the Shannon. The total tonnage carried on the canal in 1822
GRAND CANAL. See ERIE.
GRAND CAYMITE. See CAYMITES.
GRAND CHAMP, a town of France, in the dep. of Morbihan, 8 m. NNW of Vannes. Pop. 4,797.— Also a town in the dep. of Loire-Inferieure, 6 m. N of Nantes, on the Esdre rivulet. Pop. 1,446.

GRAND CHEVREUIL (PRAIRIE DU), a tract of meadow ground in Louisiana, U. S., which stretches along the E bank of the Teche, from 8 m. SE of Opelousas to about 7 m. SE of New Iberia. It is 52 m. long, with a medium breadth of 2 m. Cotton and maize are the general objects of culture; and are both produced in large quantities, and of excellent quality.
GRANDCOUR, a small town of the Swiss cant. of Vaud, near the lake of Neufchatel, 10 m. W of Friburg.
GRAND DETOUR, a village in Ogle co., in the state of Illinois, 167 m. N of Springfield, on the E side of Rock river.
GRANDE. See PARA.
GRANDE ANSE. See ANCE GRANDE.

GRANDE ISLE, an island in the St. Lawrence, fronting Catharine's town, in the S part of Beauharnois co. It is <math>4\frac{1}{2}</math> m. long by <math>1\frac{1}{2}</math> m. broad. On its S side, the Beauharnois channel presents some dangerous rapids.—Also an island in the S. Atlantic, of doubtful position, first sighted by La Roche in 1675, and laid down in charts in the parallel of 45°, but by Cook in 35° 30′ 38" W long., and by Dalrymple in 46° 30'. GRA
GRANDE - PAROISSE (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of Seine-et-Marne, cant. of Montereau, on the r. bank of the Seine. Pop. 1,256.
GRANDE-RIO. See RIO-GRANDE.
GRANDE-RIVIERE (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of Jura, cant. of Saint-Laurent. Pop. 1,005.
GRANDES VENTES (Les), a commune of France, in the dep. of Seine-Inferieure, cant. of Bellencombre. Pop. 2,015.
GRANDE - VERRIERE (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of Saone-et-Loire, cant. of Saint Leger-suis-Bevray. Pop. 1,485.
GRAND-FONTAINE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Vosges. Pop. 1,623.
GRAND GOSIER, one of the Candelarias, in N lat. 29° 50′, W long. 89° 3′, 4½ m. E of Breton kay. A great spit, with breakers, extends from this island 10 m. NNE to Log island.
GRAND GULF, a village in Claiborne co., in the state of Mississippi, 65 m. S by W of Jackson, on Grand gulf, a remarkable bend in the Mississippi river, 360 m. above New Orleans. Pop. 1,000.
GRAND HAVEN, a village in Ottawa co., in the state of Michigan, U. S., 213 m. W by N of Detroit, on the S back of Grand river, near its entrance into Lake Michigan. It has a good harbour.

GRAND ISLAND, an island in Lake Superior, on the N side of the lake.—Also an island in Niagara river, in Erie co., Canada, about 9 m. long and 6 m. broad. The south extremity is 4 m. N of Fort Erie.—Also an island at the E end of Lake Ontario, about 12 m. in length, and from 2 to 7 m. in breadth, and extending to within 3 m. of Niagara falls. It is finely wooded.—Also a county in the NW part of Vermont, consisting chiefly of two large islands in Lake Champlain, of which the Americans have changed the French name to North Hero and South Hero. North Hero is about 13 m. in length and 2 m. in breadth. South Hero is about 14 m. in length, and 33 m. in breadth. The cap. is North Hero, on the island of the same name. Vermont, a township and port of entry in the latter island, contains upwards of 500 inhabitants. Both islands are fertile, producing good crops of wheat and other grain. Pop. of co. in 1840, 3,883.

GRAND JUNCTION CANAL, a very extensive line of inland navigation, connecting the metropolis of England with the great manufacturing districts in the interior of the kingdom. It commences on the Thames at Brentford; crosses the Brent rivulet to the valley of the Colne; runs up that valley, passing Uxbridge, Rickmansworth, Walford, King's Langley, and Berkhampstead, to the summit-level at Cowroast, where it is 395 ft. above the Thames. The summit-level extends 33 m. to Tring, through a deep cutting. From Tring, the canal proceeds to Leighton - Buzzard, Fenny - Stratford, Wolverston, and Stony-Stratford, a distance of 25 m., with a descent of 192 ft. It then crosses the Ouse to Stoke-Bruern, a distance of <math>6\frac{1}{2}</math> m., in which it has a rise of 112 ft. It then passes through Blisworth tunnel, 3,080 yds. in length, and runs 13½ m. on one level. At Whitton-mill it rises 60 ft.; and 41 m. beyond this enters Braunston tunnel, 2,045 yds. in length. From this tunnel to its junction with the Oxford canal, is \frac{1}{8} m. with a fall of 37 ft. Its total length on the line now traced is 904 m.; average width 43 ft.; depth from 41 to 5 ft. It sends off a branch at Paddington 131 m. in length; another of 63 m. at Tring; another of 6 m. to Aylesbury; one of 9½ m. to Buckingham; and another of 9 m. from Gayton to the Nen near Northampton.

GRAND LAKE, a sheet of water in New Brunswick, about 45 m. from St. John, and 30 m. from Fredericton, separated from the St. John river by an alluvial tract about 1 m. wide, and communicating with it by the Gemsee, a narrow and deep channel 4 m. in length. It is 30 m. long, and from 3 to 6 m. broad; and is connected by navigable openings with French and Maquapit lakes. It is supplied by the Salmon river, which flows into its N extremity. Great quantities of fish, and especially of gaspereaux, a sort of large herring, are caught in this lake.
GRAND LEMPS, a small town of Dauphiny, in the dep. of the Isere, 6 m. from La Cote St. Andre. Pop. 1,700.

GRAND LIEU, a lake of France, in the dep. of Loire-Inferieure, 6 m. SW of Nantes. It is 8 m. in length, and 4 m. in average breadth. The Boulogne enters it on the S, and the Ognon on the E; and it discharges its waters by the Achenau, into the Loire.
GRAND LUCE', a canton and town of France, in the dep. of the Sarthe, and 15 m. SE of Le Mans, on the Veuve. Pop. of town, 2,372; of cant., 10,955.
GRAND MANAN, an island 6 m. S by SE of Campo-Bello island, in the Atlantic, opposite to Passamaquoddy bay. It is 14 m. in length, and 7 m. in breadth, well-timbered, and rises boldly from the water.
GRANDOLA, a town of Portugal, in Estremadura, 32 m. SE of Setubal. Pop. 2,185.
GRANDOON, a small low island in Miramichi bay, in the gulf of St. Lawrence, 2 m. ENE 1 E of Oak point.
GRAND PRAIRIE, a township in Marion co., in Ohio, U. S. Pop. 717.

GRAND-PRE', a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Ardennes, 30 m. SSE of Mezieres, on the r. bank of the Aire. Pop. of com., 1,300; of cant., 9,591.—Also a seigniory in the co. of St. Maurice, Lower Canada, on the N side of Lake St. Peter.
GRANDRIEU, a canton and town of France, in the dep. of Lozere, 12 m. WNW of Langogne. Pop. of cant. 5,483; of town, 1,462.
GRANDRIFS, a village of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, cant. of Saint-Antheme. Pop. 1,163.
GRANDRIS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Rhone, cant. of Saint-Niziers-d'Azerques. Pop. 1,725.

GRAND RIVER, a river of the United States, which rises in Iowa territory, flows SE, and enters the Missouri about 250 m. from its mouth. It is between 80 and 100 yds. wide where it joins the Missouri.—Also a river of Louisiana, which enters the Arkansaw about 700 m. from the Mississippi. It is 130 yds. wide at its mouth. Below the influx of this river commence the rapids, which continue for several miles down the Arkansaw.—Also a large river of Louisiana, which falls into the Osage, one of the tributary branches of the Missouri.—Also a river of Michigan, U. S., which has its rise in lakes and ponds in the SE corner of the territory; and is composed of the main head-streams which unite in Jackson co. The combined stream flows NW and W, and falls into Lake Michigan on the E bank, about 20 m. to the N of Raisin river, after a course of 270 m., through a country consisting alternately of woodland and meadow. It is navigable for steamboats 40 m. to the Grand rapids; and for boats to near its source.—Also a river of Ohio, which, flowing S, falls into Lake Erie 80 m. SW of Presque isle. It has a winding course, and rapid current, and is not navigable.—Also a river of Canada, which runs into the Detroit in N lat. 42° 34'.—Also a river of Canada, which runs into the St. Lawrence in N lat. 47° 3'.

GRAND RIVER, or Ouse, a river of Upper Canada, which rises in the township of Amaranth, in Wellington district, and runs S and SE to Lake GRA Erie. Its principal tributaries are the Speed and the Canastoga. It is navigable for large vessels as far as Dunnville, and for boats to near Brantford, 60 m. above Dunnville, where a canal, 3 m. in length, overcomes a fall of 33 ft. in the river, and opens communication with Brantford. The land along the river is undulating; the timber is a mixture of hardwood and pine. The towns and villages of Dunnville-Cayuga, Indiana, York, Seneca, Caledonia, Brantford, Paris, Galt, Preston, Elora, and Fergus, are situated on the G. river. The Indians of the Six Nations had a grant of land on this river, 6 m. in depth on each side, from Lake Erie to its head, in 1784. Of this territory, amounting to 694,810 acres, the greater part has since been surrendered to the Crown, and sold for the benefit of these tribes, consisting of Mohawks, Oneidas, Senecas, Onondagas, Cayugas, Delawares, and some other families, whose numbers in 1843 amounted to 2,223, in 500 families. The greater part of these Indians live in log houses, scattered over both sides of the river over a district of about 220,000 acres. They are also collected in 3 small villages called Mohawk, Tuscarora, and Cayuga. A large majority of them profess Christianity.

GRAND RIVER, a township in Caldwell co., in Missouri, U. S. Pop. 302.—Also a township in Livingston co., in Missouri. Pop. 173.-Also a township in Van Buren co., Missouri. Pop. 650.— Also a township in Carroll co., in Missouri. Pop. 1,064.—Also a township in Henry co., Missouri. Pop. 999.
GRAND RIVIERE, or QUIAUKSQUACK, a river of Lower Canada, which rises near the head-waters of the Restigouche, and runs SW into the St. John. 5 m. above the great falls.
GRANDE-RIVIERE-DU-CHENE. See BELLE RIVIERE.
GRAND RUISSEAU (LE), a small river of Lower Canada, rising in the mountains which border the St. Lawrence to the S, and falling into that river about 40 m. below the town of Orleans.
GRAND-SERRE (LE), a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Drome, cant. and 25 m. NE of Valence. Pop. of cant. 12,342; of com. 1,785.

GRAND TRAVERSE, a bay on the NE side of Lake Michigan, about 12 m. long, and between 4 m. and 5 m. broad.—Also a range of islands at the entrance into Green bay, in Lake Michigan.-Also a river in the Michigan territory, U.S., which enters Lake Michigan, between Marguerite and Michilimackinack.
GRAND-VAL, a commune of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, cant. of Saint-Amand-Roche- Savine. Pop. 1,000.
GRAND VARRE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Aveyron, cant. of Couques, on the Dourdon. Pop. 1,087.
GRAND-VIEW, a township in Washington co., in Ohio, 141 m. SE of Columbus, on the Ohio river, 25 m. above Marietta. Pop. 514.-Also a village in Edgar co., Illinois, 114 m. E of Springfield.
GRANDVILLARS, a town of France, in the dep. of Haut-Rhin, on the Allain, 9 m. S by E of Belfort.
GRANDVILLE, a village in Kent co., in Michigan, 175 m. W by N of Detroit. Pop. 200.—Also a seigniory of Lower Canada, in the co. of Kamouraska, fronting the St. Lawrence.

GRANDVILLERS, a town of France, in the dep. of Vosges, 17 m. NNW of Beauvais. Pop. 1,091. GRANE, GRAINE, or GRAN, a port of Arabia, on the Persian gulf, 12 or 13 leagues SW of Core Abdillah. The harbour is large and well-sheltered, and has sufficient water for large ships. The country in the vicinity is low and partially cultivated. | The inhabitants of G. trade with the Red sea, Scinde, 107. and Guzerat; and import grain, coffee, and Indian produce for the supply of the interior.

GRANGE, a parish in the lower district of Banffshire, 16 m. SW of Banff, extending along the banks of the Isla in three long ridges. Pop. in 1831, 333; in 1841, 1,661.—Also a township in the p. of West Kirby, Cheshire, 81 m. N of Great Neston. Area 1,180 acres. Pop. 132.—Also a hamlet in the lathe of Aylesford, Kent. Pop. 157.—Also a parish, 21/4 m. N of the city of Armagh, co. Armagh. Area 6,794 acres. Pop. in 1831, 4,132; in 1841, 3,823.— Also a parish, 3\frac{3}{4} m. SW of the city of Kilkenny, co. Kilkenny. Area 1,934 acres. Pop. in 1841, 542.— Also a parish, 42 m. WSW of the town of Kilconnel, co. Galway. Area 4,668 acres. Pop. in 1831, 1,045; in 1841, 1,038.—Also a village in the p. of Pollrone, co. Kilkenny, 32 m. SE of Pilltown. Pop. 240.—Also a parish in co. Limerick. Area 2,839 acres. Pop. in 1831, 721; in 1841, 708.—Also a village in the p. of Ahamplish, co. Sligo, near the head of the Mile harbour, 73 m. N of Sligo. Pop. 140.—Also a village in the p. of Desertcreight, co. Tyrone. Pop. 146.—Also a parish in Queen's co., 43 m. N of Carlow. Area 863 acres. Pop. 276.
GRANGE (LA), a cape on the N coast of Hayti, in N lat. 19° 54′ 45″, W long. 71° 38′ 39″.
GRANGE (LA VILLE), a commune of France, in the dep. of Haute-Saone, cant. of Villersexel. Pop. 1,097. Straw-hats are largely made here.
GRANGECLOVAN, a parish in co. Kilkenny, situated on the E lip of the Nore, and traversed by the road from Ballyragget to Kilkenny. Area 3,886 acres. Pop. in 1831, 949; in 1841, 1,224.
GRANGEFORD, or GRANGEFORTH, a parish in co. Carlow, 23 m. NW by W of Tullow. Area 3,504 acres. Pop. in 1831, 926; in 1841, 1,027.
GRANGEGEETH, a parish in co. Meath, 21 m. N of Slane. Area 4,447 acres. Pop. in 1831, 1,304; in 1841, 1,352.
GRANGE-KILREE, or KILREE-GRANGE, a parish in co. Kilkenny, 33 m. SSE of Kilkenny. Area 992 acres. Pop. in 1831, 145; in 1841, 205.
GRANGEMOCKLER, or NINE-MILE-HOUSE, a parish in co. Tipperary, 7 m. N of Carrick-on-Suir. Area 2,803\(\frac{1}{2}\) acres. Pop. in 1841, 1,106.

GRANGEMOUTH, a thriving and important seaport in the p. of Falkirk, Stirlingshire, at the confluence of Grange-burn with the Carron, a m. above the influx of the united streams into the Forth. Its resources and capacities as a port depend mainly on its commanding the entrance, through the mouth of Grange-burn, of the Forth and Clyde canal. It has a dry dock, commodious quays, and lofty extensive storehouses. The Carron company have here a spacious wharf. Rope-making and ship-building employ a number of hands. Previous to 1810, all vessels belonging to the port were registered at Borrowstounness: G., however, has now a customhouse of its own. The total amount of customs received at the port of G. in 1850 was £15,873. Timber, hemp, flax, tallow, deals, and iron from the Baltic, and grain from foreign countries, and from the E coast of Scotland and England, are landed on its quays. Pop. of the town in 1831, 1,155; in 1841, 1,488. GRANGE OF ST. JOHN BAPTIST, a parish in co. Tipperary, 2 m. S of Fethard. Area 2,857 acres. Pop. in 1831, 771; in 1841, 782.
GRANGER, a township of Alleghany co., in the state of N. Carolina, U. S., 23 m. SW of Exeter. Pop. 1,064.—Also a township in Medina co., Ohio, 131 m. NE of Columbus. Pop. 956.
GRANGEROSNOLVIN, or GRANGE-MONACHORUM, a parish in co. Kildare, 32 m. ESE of Athy. III. GRA Area 1,393 acres. Pop. in 1831, 114; in 1841,

GRANGE-SILVIA, or GRANGE-SYLVÆ, a parish on the E border of co. Kilkenny, Leinster, containing the town, and sometimes bearing the name of Goresbridge. Area 4,796 acres. Pop. in 1831, 2,313; in 1841, 2,413.
GRANGES (LES), a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Aube, cant. of Romilly-sur-Seine. Pop. 580 .- Also a commune in the dep. of Vosges, 12 m. SW of Saint-Die. Pop. 2,365.
GRANGES - DE - CHATILLON, a village of France, in the dep. of Doubs, cant. and 6 m. ESE of Quingey.
GRANICA, a village of Poland, in the gov. of Sandomir, 29 m. ENE of Radom.
GRANIER (DENT DE), a summit of the Alps, between the frontiers of the French dep. of Isere and of Savoy, 7 m. S of Chambery, in N lat. 45° 27' 50". Alt. 6,358 ft.
GRANITE, a village of Sicily, 31 m. SW of Messina, at the foot of Ætna.
GRANITEVILLE, a township in Edgefield district, in the state of S. Carolina, U. S. A joint stock company have recently founded a considerable manufactory here of drillings, sheetings, and shirtings.
GRANITOLA (CAPE), a low sandy projection on the coast of Sicily, the SW extremity of the prov. of Trapani, in N lat. 37° 34'.
GRANJA, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Ceara, on the l. bank of the Camercina, 30 m. NNE of Villa-de-Vicosa.
GRANJA (LA), a town of Spain, in the prov. and 36 m. S of Badajoz. Pop. 2,246.
GRANNA, a town of Russian Poland, in the gov. of Grodno, on the Bug, 34 m. SW of Bielsk.
GRANNE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Drome, cant. of Crest. Pop. 1,684.
GRANNOCH (Loch), a sequestered and romantic lake, in the N extremity of the parish of Girthon, in Kirkcudbrightshire, about 3 m. in length, and a m. broad.
GRANOLLERS, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 15 m. NE of Barcelona. Pop. 2,350. It has some woollen-weaving establishments.
GRANON, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 33 m. W of Logrono. Pop. 1,270.

GRANS, a town of France, in the dep. of Bouchesdu-Rhone, 3 m. SW of Salon. Pop. 1,780.—Also a small town of Spain, in Aragon, district of Bibagorza, at the confluence of the two small rivers Essera and Isacenna, 14 m. NNE of Balbastro.
GRANSDON (GREAT and LITTLE), two parishes in Huntingdonshire, 7 m. SE of St. Neot's. Area of Great G., 3,200 acres. Pop. 622. Area of Little G., 1,896 acres. Pop. 273.
GRANSEE, a town of Prussia in Brandenburg, 18 m. ENE of New Ruppin. Pop. 2,666.
GRANSO, an island in the Baltic, near the E coast of Sweden, in N lat. 57° 46'.

GRANSON, or GRANSEE, a small town of the Swiss cant. of Vaud, on the lake and 6 m. SW of Neufchatel. It is meanly built, but memorable for a victory obtained here in 1476, over the troops of Charles the Bold, duke of Burgundy.
GRANSTON, a parish in Pembrokeshire, 5 m. WSW of Fishguard. Pop. 164.—Also a small lake of N. Carolina, 25 m. W of Exeter.

GRANT, a county in the N part of Kentucky, U. S., intersected by Eagle creek, an affluent of Kentucky river, Area 184 sq. m. Pop. 4,191. The cap. is Williamstown.-Also a county in the central part of Iowa, watered by the Missisinewa, a branch of the Wabash. Area 415 sq. m. Pop. 4,875. Its cap. is Marion.—Also a county in the SW part of Wisconsin territory. Area 1,200 sq. m. Pop. 3,926. Its cap. is Lancaster.
GRANTA. See CAM.
GRANTCHESTER, a parish of Cambridgeshire, 2. m. SW of Cambridge. Area 1,591 acres. Pop. 606.

GRANTHAM, a borough, market-town, and parish, in the co. and 25 m. SSW of Lincoln. Area of p., 4,320 acres. Pop. in 1801, including the townships of Harrowby, Spittlegate, Houghton with Walton, and Manthorp with Little Gonerby, 4,288; in 1831, 7,427; in 1841, 5,560. The church is a beautiful structure, with a lofty tower surmounted by a spire rising to the height of 273 ft.—The town is situated on the ancient Roman road called Irminstreet, W of the Witham, and to the E is the Grantham and Nottingham canal. The streets are wellpaved, cleaned, and lighted with gas. Spittlegate on the S, and Gonerby on the N, are so connected with G., that they may be considered as constituting one town. The principal local trade is that of malting and exporting corn, the produce of the surrounding agricultural district. The boundaries of the parl. borough are limited to the town, with Spittlegate, and Manthorpe-with-Little-Gonerby. Electors in 1837, 678; in 1848, 721.—The G. canal, from this town to the river Trent, which it joins nearly opposite the mouth of the Nottingham canal, is 33 m. in length, with a fall of 147 ft. It passes Harlaxton, Slainwith-Close, and Cropwell-Butler; and sends off a branch to the town of Bingham.

GRANTHAM, a township of Upper Canada, on the S side of Lake Ontario, 30 m. S of Toronto, intersected by the Welland canal. Pop. in 1842, 3,832. The town of St. Catharine's is the principal place in this township. - Also a township in Sullivan co., in New Hampshire, U. S., 54 m. WNW of Concord. Pop. 1,036.
GRANTHAM ISLAND, an island on the S coast of New Holland, in Port Lincoln. It is nearly 1 m. in length, and is joined to Surfleet-point by a shoal.
GRANT ISLAND, an island of Australia, in Bass strait, in Port Western. Its SE extremity has an alt. of 480 ft.
GRANTLEY, a township in the p. of Ripon, W. R. of Yorkshire, 51 m. WSW of Ripon. Pop., with Skeldin, in 1831, 243; in 1841, 246.

GRANTLEY HARBOUR, an inlet on the coast of Russian America, E of Port Clarence, in N lat. <math>65^{\circ} 16' 40''</math>. It is 10 m. in length, and <math>2\frac{1}{4}</math> m. in breadth, and forms one of the safest wintering stations in the vicinity of Behring's straits. It was first entered and named by Captain Beechey in 1827.

GRANTON, an incipient town and sea-port on the frith of Forth; 21 m. from Edinburgh, in the p. of Cramond. A magnificent pier, forming decidedly the best harbour in the frith of Forth, has been constructed here by the duke of Buccleugh. It is 1,700 ft. in length, and from 80 to 160 ft. in breadth; four pairs of jetties, each running out 90 ft., go off at intervals of 350 ft.; and 2 slips, each 325 ft. long, facilitate the shipping and the landing of cattle, and railway coaches and waggons. The depth of water around it, and by means of its low-water jetties, affords access, in any state of tide, for steamers and ships of even the largest size. The Edinburgh, Perth, and Dundee railway has its ferry across the frith between G. and Burntisland, a breadth of 5 m. G. is 29 m. from Cupar by this line; 44 m. from Dundee; and 42 m. from Perth.

GRANTOWN, a village in the p. of Cromdale, Inverness-shire, 22 m. S of Forres; and 1 m. S of Castle-Grant, on the l. side of the river Spey, on the road from the lower to the higher districts of the country. Pop. 814. GRA
GRANUCILLO, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Zamora, 10 m. W of Benavente. Pop. 300.
GRANVELLE, a small town of France, in the dep. of Haute-Saone, 11 m. SW of Vesoul. Pop. 400.

GRANVILLE, a seaport of France, in the dep. of La Manche, at the mouth of the Bosq, 12 m. NW of Avranches, in N lat. 48° 50′ 7″, W long. 1° 35′ 57". Pop. in 1831, 7,350; in 1841, 8,347. It stands on a rocky peninsula, joined to the continent by a narrow neck of land. It has a considerable traffic, particularly with the neighbouring island of Jersey; and sends out a number of vessels to Newfoundland and the whale fishery.—The cant. comprises 8 com. Pop. in 1841, 16,964.

GRANVILLE, a county in the N part of North Carolina, U. S., on Tar river. Area 828 sq. m. Pop. 18,817. The cap. is Oxford.—Also a township in Addison co., in Vermont, 35 m. SSW of Montpelier. Pop. 545.—Also a township in the state of New York, in Washington co., 63 m. NNE of Albany. Pop. 3,846.—Also a township in Hampden co., in Massachusetts, 120 m. S by W of Boston. Pop. 1,414.—Also a township in Bradford co., in Pennsylvania, 147 m. NE of Harrisburg. Pop. 651.— Also a township in Mifflin co., in Pennsylvania. Pop. 1,016.—Also a township in Licking co., in Ohio, 34 m. NE of Columbus. Pop. 1,528. In the neighbourhood is a Baptist college.—Also a village in Monongalia co., in Virginia, on Dunkard creek. Pop. 150.—Also a v. in Delaware co., in Iowa, 68 m. NE of Indianapolis.—Also a township in Milwaukie co., in Wisconsin territory. Pop. 225.-Also a co. in the Johnstown district of Upper Canada, comprising the townships of Augusta, Edwardsburgh, S. Gower, Oxford, and Wolford. Pop. in 1836, 17,160.
GRANVILLE'S RIVER, a river of the island of Egmont, or New Guernsey.

GRANVILLIERS, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Oise, arrond. of Beauvais. —The cant., comprising 23 com., had a pop. of 13,222 in 1841.—The town, 18 m. NNW of Beauvais, had a pop. of 1,861. It has considerable manufactures of serges.
GRANYENA, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Lerida, 4 m. SW of Cervera.
GRANZE-DI-VESCOVANA, a town of Austrian Lombardy, in the prov. and 21 m. SSW of Padua. Pop. 1,425.

GRAO, a small seaport on the E coast of Spain, at the mouth of the river Guadalaviar, 3 m. E of Valencia. Pop. 5,000. It exports wine, silk, wool, dried fruits and soda. A railway is in progress [1851] between G. and Valencia. It crosses the Turia by a viaduct 900 ft. long.
GRAPE ISLAND, an island of Upper Canada, in the bay of Quinte, at the mouth of the river Trent, in Lake Ontario, to the N of Big island.
GRAPPENHALL, a parish of the co. of Chester, 3 m. SE of Warrington. Area 2,550 acres. Pop. in 1831, 2,607; in 1841, 2,948.
GRARAH, a village of the Bled-el-Jerid, in Algiers, 60 m. NW of Wergela, on the route from Marocco to Tripoli.
GRAS (Les), a commune of France, in the dep. of Doubs, cant. of Bourg-Saint-Andeol, on the Negue. Pop. 831.

GRASELITZ, or GRASLITZ, a mining-town of Bohemia, on the Zwoda, 19 m. NE of Eger, and 88 m. NW of Prague. Pop. 4,790. It has some cotton manufactures, and a large quantity of musical instruments and copper utensils are made here.
GRASGARD, a small town of Sweden, on the SE coast of Oland island.

GRASHOLM, an island off the coast of Wales, in N lat. 51° 43′ 54″, 6 m. WNW of Skomer island. It is about 3 m. in circumf., and rises 146 ft. above low water.—Also one of the smaller Orkney islands, a 1 m. S of Shapinsha.

GRASMERE, a parish in Westmoreland, 3½ m. WNW of Ambleside, romantically situated on the small river Rother, which connects the Grasmere, Rydal, and Winandermere lakes. Area 24,550 acres. Pop. of p. in 1801, returned under the chapelry of Ambleside and the townships of G., Langdales. Rydal, and Loughbrigg, 1,338; in 1831, 2,083; in 1841, 1,681. G. lake, and the deep vale in which it is situated, are universally admired for their peculiar beauty. The vale is about 4 m. in circumf., "hedged in by mountains, whose heads, rude, craggy, and fantastic, give all the force and beauty of contrast to the smiling meads and peaceful dwellings which rest beneath their feet." The lake is only about 11 m. in circumf. It has a well defined and beautifully embayed margin, "with eminences; some of rock, some of soft turf, that half-conceal and vary the figure of the little lake they command. From the shore a low promontory pushes itself into the water, and on it stands the v., with the church rising in the midst of it." The poet Wordsworth is buried in the churchyard of this secluded village.

GRASMERE-FELL, a mountain of Cumberland, situated near Crummock-water. It forms rather a vast ridge than a pointed summit; and is connected with two or three other mountains of inferior dignity. It rises 2,756 ft. above sea-level.

GRASS, or LA GRAS, a considerable river of the state of New York, U. S., in St. Lawrence co., which runs NW and NE, and enters the St. Lawrence near the head of Great St. Regis island, after a course of about 125 m.
GRASSANO, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Basilicata, 16 m. W of Matera. Pop. 3,370.
GRASSBY, a parish in Lincolnshire, 3 m. NW of Caistor. Area 1,720 acres. Pop. in 1831, 287; in 1841, 374.

GRASSE, an arrondissement, canton, and town of France, in the dep. of Var.—The arrond. comprises the 8 cants. of Antibes, Saint-Auban, Bar, Cannes, Coursegoulen, G., Saint-Vallier, and Vence. Area 114,285 hect. Pop. in 1841, 65,164; in 1846, 66,150.—The cant., comprising 4 com., had a pop. of 11,197 in 1846.—The town is situated 23 m. NNE of Draguignan, and 10 m. N of Cannes. Pop. in 1789, 9,178; in 1846, 6,706. It is neatly built, on the side of a mountain, in an amphitheatre of gardens appropriated to the culture of roses, orange-trees, heliotropes, and other odoriferous plants, made use of in the manufacture of perfumery, for which the place is in high repute. Vines and olives are likewise cultivated in the neighbourhood, and a considerable quantity of fruit, which is dried and exported. G. has besides a traffic in silk, leather, honey, soap, and liqueurs of different kinds.
GRASSE (LA), a town of France, in the dep. of Aude, 23 m. SW of Narbonne. Pop. 1,320.
GRASSINGTON, a township in the p. of Linton, W. R. of Yorkshire, 8 m. NNE of Skipton. Area 4,960 acres. Pop. in 1831, 1,067; in 1841, 1,056.
GRASSTHORPE, a township in Marnham p., in the co. of Nottingham, 41 m. SE by E of Tuxford. Area 510 acres. Pop. in 1831, 118; in 1841, 94.
GRATCHEVSKAIA, a fort of Russia, on the Volga, in the gov. and 125 m. NW of Astrakhan.
GRATCHEVSKOI, a fort of Asiatic Russia, in the gov. of Omsk, 75 m. WNW of Semipalatinsk, near the r. bank of the Irtisch.
GRATELOUPE, a village of France, in the dep. of Lot-et-Garonne, cant. and 6 m. WNW of Castel- Moron.
GRATELY, a parish in Southamptonshire, 6½ m. マングス カンプランプラング カング タング アング ディー・ディー クリー・ディー・ディー・ディー・ディー・ディー・ディー・ディー・ディー・ディー・ディ GRA WSW of Andover. Area 980 acres. Pop. in 1831, 130; in 1841, 141.
GRATIEN (SAINT), a small town of France, in the dep. of Seine-et-Oise, cant. and 2 m. SW of Montmorency. Pop. 400.
GRATIOSA. See GRACIOSA.
GRATIOT, a county in the central part of Michigan, intersected by Maple and Pine rivers. Area 576 sq. m.—Also a village in Hopewell township, Licking co., Ohio, 42 m. E of Columbus. Pop. 300.
GRATIOT (FORT), a fort in Michigan, U. S., occupying a commanding position at the outlet of the St. Clair from Lake Huron.
GRATIS, a township in Preble co., in Ohio, U. S., 92 m. W of Columbus. Pop. 1,931.
GRATSANITZA, a town of Bosnia, 10 m. WSW of Zvornik, on the r. bank of the Spressa.
GRATTERI, a town of Sicily, in the prov. of Palermo, cant. and 7 m. SSW of Cefalu.
GRATTWEIN, a town of Styria, in the circle and 7 m. NW of Grätz, near the r. bank of the Mühr. Pop. 525. Tripoli and chalk are quarried in the environs.
GRATWICH, a parish in Staffordshire, 4 m. W by S of Uttoxeter. Area 800 acres. Pop. in 1831, 116; in 1841, 119.

GRATZ, one of the circles, or administrative provinces, into which the duchy of Styria is divided. It comprises the N part of Lower Styria, on both sides of the Mühr, and has an area of 102.4 German sq. m. Pop. in 1837, 341,945. The surface is hilly, but there are few very high mountains. The valleys are fertile and picturesque, particularly along the Mühr, between G. and Bruck. The principal rivers are the Mühr, the Fistritz, and the Raab. The cattle, milk, butter, and cheese of G. are in great repute. The hills contain large forests of pine; but the chief riches of this circle, as of Styria in general, are its copper, lead, and iron-mines, and the manufacture of its mineral products.—The gov. of G., as distinct from the circle, comprises the whole duchy of Styria. Area 407.6 German sq. m. Pop. in 1837, 935,576. GRATZ, OF BAYERISCHGRATZ, [VEND. Gradez; SLAV. Nimetski-Grad,] an ancient and considerable town of Styria, the cap. of the above circle, situated on the river Mühr, which rushes impetuously through the town, at an alt. of 1,486 ft. above sea-level, in N lat. 47° 4′, E long. 15° 26′. Pop. in 1837, 43,798. The ranges of hills which from Brück face the valley of the Mühr, open out at the S extremity of the valley to the E and W, leaving between them an oblong plain about 20 m. in length from N to S, and averaging 5 m. in breadth. Towards the N part of this plain, and close at the foot of the E range, stands the city of G. The ancient part of the town, which is enclosed by ramparts and a glacis, is small; but the extent of building in the suburbs is very considerable. Its Schlossberg or citadel is built on a very steep hill on the banks of the Mühr, and might still form a fortification but for the extent of the suburbs. Its slopes have been cut into pleasing walks; and its summit is discrowned of tower and keep. G. has been since 1787 the see of a bishop. It was the seat of a university from 1586 to 1782, when its place was supplied by a lyceum, an academy, and a large school. In 1827, however, the university was re-established. Its library contains 40,000 vols. It is also the seat of a theological seminary, an Adelige damenstift or chapter of noble ladies, and numerous hospitals. It is the military cap. or seat of military government for Tyrol, Carinthia, and Carniola. The houses in G. are of stone, and the town is in general well-built, but the streets are narrow and dark. In the old central part are some curious edifices, and one or two noble palaces. The palace of Count Altern is a very noble edifice. G. has 22 churches and chapels great and small, and a chain-bridge across the Mühr, 318 ft. long, by which it communicates with the suburb of Muhrstadt. The mausoleum erected in the church of St. Catherine to the emperor Ferdinand II., who was a native of G., is remarkable for the ornamental sculpture of its interior. The theatre, the barracks, and the arsenal, are all entitled to attention; also a museum for the antiquities of Styria, founded in 1812 by the archduke John, and from him called the Johanneum.—G. communicates by railway with Vienna on the N, from which it is 1224 m. distant by rail; and with Cilly on the S, from which it is 87 m. distant. It possesses several manufactories of hardware, stoneware, and saltpetre, also of cotton and silk; and forms the centre of the inland trade between Germany and Italy.—The environs are very attractive. From the wide plain all around rise shaggy wooded acclivities, and hills broken into wide patches of pasture, with gardens hanging on their skirts.—The climate is cold in winter, descending to 10° and even 8°; while the extreme heat of summer is often 90°.

GRATZ, or BÖHMISCH GRATZEN, a town of Bohemia, in the circle and 16 m. SE of Budweis, on the r. bank of the Danko. Pop. 1,586. It has manufactories of glass and of crystal, which it exports in considerable quantities.
GRATZ, or Grodzisko, a small town of Prussian Poland, 22 m. SW of Posen. Pop. 3,000.
GRATZ, a small town of Austrian Silesia, on the r. bank of the Mora, 4 m. S of Troppau.
GRAUBUNDEN. See Grisons.

GRAUDENZ, or GRUDZIADZ, a considerable town of Prussia, at the confluence of the Vistula and the Ossa, on the r. bank of the Vistula, here 2,700 ft. wide, in N lat. 53° 29', E long. 18° 45'; 30 m. SSE of Marienwerder, 14 m. NNE of Culm. Pop. in 1837, 5,918. It is enclosed with a high wall, and has 3 gates. It has manufactories of cloth, extensive breweries, and some trade in corn and tobacco. The chief part of the inhabitants are German Lutherans. A strong fort was erected here, on the bank of the Vistula, about a m. to the N of the town, in 1770-6; and considerable outworks have been since added. In 1798 a bridge of boats was laid across the river at this town, but it has been transferred to Kuzelrack.

GRAULHET, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Tarn, arrond. of Lavaur. Pop. of cant., comprising 7 com., 10,135.—The town is situated on the l. bank of the Adon, 11 m. ENE of Lavaur. Pop. 5,167.
GRAULIE'RE (LA), a town of France, in the dep. of Corrèze, cant. of Seilhac, 9 m. NW of Tulle. Pop. 1,600.
GRAUPEN, a town of Bohemia, in the circle of Leitmeritz. Pop. 1,408.
GRAUS, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 41 m. E of Huesca, at the confluence of the Esera and the Isavena. Pop. 2,380.
GRAVALOS, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 30 m. SE of Logrono. Pop. 1,015.

GRAVE, a town of Belgium, in N. Brabant, on the l. bank of the Maese, 19 m. ENE of Bois-le-Duc. Pop. 2,830. It is fortified in the modern style, and made a gallant defence against the French in the winter of 1794-5.
GRAVE CREEK, a river of Virginia, U. S., which runs into the Ohio near Elizabeth Town, after a course of 24 m.
GRAVE (LA), a canton and town of France, in the dep. of Hautes-Alpes, 18 m. NW of Briançon. Pop. of cant. 2,304; of town 1,886.

GRAVE-EN-OYSANS (LA), a town of France, in the dep. of Hautes-Alpes, 36 m. NNE of Gap. Pop. 1,500. GRAVEDONA, or GRAVIDONA, a small town of Austrian Italy, on the W bank of Lake Como, 42 m. N of Milan. Pop. 1,400.

GRAVEL (GREAT), a river of North America, which enters the Osage 118 m. above the confluence of that river with the Missouri. - Little G. river has its sources near the Missouri, and running SE enters the Osage on the L bank, 20 m. below the Great G.
GRAVELAINA, a small town on the SW coast of the island of Negropont, on the site of the ancient Eretria, 13 m. SE of Negropont.
